birth.notes: WikiTree gives Feb 1, 1720-1 (Old Style/New Style transition); 1721 New Style.

death.cause: Camp fever (typhus epidemicus) contracted during Siege of Boston while serving as Captain of 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment, Connecticut Militia

burial.plot_notes: Buried near Jamaica Plain where the regiment was encamped during siege operations.

military_service.unit: 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment, Connecticut Militia (19th Regiment)
military_service.notes: Commissioned Ensign of the 4th Company in the 6th Regiment May 1764; Lieutenant October 1768; Captain of the same May 1772. Captain of the 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment in service 1776 — Siege of Boston, encamped at Jamaica Plain. Died of camp fever (typhus) Mar 7, 1776 at Roxbury Mass. DAR Ancestor #A049261. Will dated Feb 8, 1773, proved May 9, 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: Camp fever killed him at Siege of Boston
narrative_blocks.body: He died March 7, 1776 at Roxbury MA of camp fever — epidemic typhus — while serving as Captain of the 6th Company, Col Wolcott's CT Militia Regiment encamped at Jamaica Plain during the Siege of Boston. He was fifty-five and never saw the Declaration his fellow officers would sign four months later.

narrative_blocks.header: Will mentioned enslaved man named Newport
narrative_blocks.body: His will dated February 8, 1773 — three years before his Revolutionary death — named 'my negro man named Newport' alongside his wife and ten surviving children. The Connecticut Militia captain who died for the Revolution himself held a person enslaved, the contradiction documented in the same will that probated his patriot service.

narrative_blocks.header: Three children died in his epidemic year 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Within months of his March 7 death, his eldest son Jonathan died unmarried October 1, 1776 at age thirty, and his fifteen-year-old daughter Jerusha died September 26, 1776. The Hale household at Glastonbury lost father, son, and daughter in the same Revolutionary year.

birth.notes: WikiTree gives Feb 1, 1720-1 (Old Style/New Style transition); 1721 New Style.

death.cause: Camp fever (typhus epidemicus) contracted during Siege of Boston while serving as Captain of 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment, Connecticut Militia

burial.plot_notes: Buried near Jamaica Plain where the regiment was encamped during siege operations.

military_service.unit: 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment, Connecticut Militia (19th Regiment)

Wolcott's Regiment in service 1776 — Siege of Boston, encamped at Jamaica Plain. Died of camp fever (typhus) Mar 7, 1776 at Roxbury Mass. DAR Ancestor #A049261. Will dated Feb 8, 1773, proved May 9, 1776.

He was fifty-five and never saw the Declaration his fellow officers would sign four months later.

The Connecticut Militia captain who died for the Revolution himself held a person enslaved, the contradiction documented in the same will that probated his patriot service.

narrative_blocks.body: Within months of his March 7 death, his eldest son Jonathan died unmarried October 1, 1776 at age thirty, and his fifteen-year-old daughter Jerusha died September 26, 1776. The Hale household at Glastonbury lost father, son, and daughter in the same Revolutionary year.

parents.father_research_notes: Col. Thomas Welles of Glastonbury CT.

marriages.notes: Stage 6Y-202: eldest surviving child TD0238 built (Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones, m. Amasa Jones 1794, → Wilkes-Barre PA 1816). 8 other M2 children deferred (Talcott gen 6 cohort, mostly died young): Septimus 1773-1776, George Washington Huntington 1776-1777, Septimus George 1778-1844, Hannah 1779-1794, Henry 1781-1806, Lucretia Norton 1783-1876, Mercy Penelope 1788-1794, James 1790-1793.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died of typhus at Siege of Boston
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt Jonathan Hale Jr died of camp fever at Roxbury MA on March 7, 1776 — less than four years after her August 1772 marriage to Joseph Huntington. Her older brother Jonathan died unmarried that October and her younger sister Jerusha died that September, leaving her mother widowed with three children buried in one Revolutionary year.

narrative_blocks.header: Six of nine children predeceased her
narrative_blocks.body: Of the nine children she bore Rev Joseph Huntington between 1773 and 1790, six died before she did — Septimus (age 3), George Washington (age 1), Hannah (age 15), Penelope (age 6), James (age 3), and Henry (age 25 same year as her own death). Only Elizabeth, Septimus George, and Lucretia outlived their mother to maturity.

death.cause: Complication of diseases per contemporary account

education.notes: Yale College B.A. 1762. Entered at age 23 after his father had destined him for the clothier trade against his own wishes; his pastor Rev. Ebenezer Devotion encouraged the late preparation. Graduated honorably.

career.notes: Installed June 29, 1763 — within one year of his Yale graduation. Continued as pastor until his death thirty-one years later. Under his ministry the parish built the best meeting house in the region for $5,000 — services at his ordination were held in the open air either because the existing meeting house was too small or too dilapidated.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1762; D.D. honoris causa 1780; Trustee 1780-1788.

notable.notable_blurb: Coventry minister who posthumously published Universalism

cross_connections.display_label: Tutored Nathan Hale at Coventry for Yale entrance

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter burned most copies of his Universalist treatise
narrative_blocks.body: 'Calvinism Improved' — his posthumous 1796 treatise defending universal salvation and denying eternal punishment — was doomed to limited circulation because one of his daughters consigned the greater part of the edition to the flames. Sprague's Annals of the American Pulpit notes 'so effectual was the suppression of the work that it is now almost impossible to find a copy.'

narrative_blocks.header: Son from first marriage became Ohio Governor
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest son with Hannah Devotion — Samuel H. Huntington — was orphaned at age six when Hannah died in 1771 and was adopted by his uncle Samuel Huntington, the Connecticut Governor and Declaration signer. The boy grew up to attend Dartmouth and Yale, settled Cleveland in 1801, and became the third Governor of Ohio 1808-1810 — half-brother to the nine children Joseph would later have with second wife Elizabeth Hale.

narrative_blocks.header: Tutored Nathan Hale of Coventry for Yale
narrative_blocks.body: He prepared Nathan Hale (1755-1776) of Coventry for Yale before Hale entered the college in 1769 and graduated 1773. Hale was hanged at New York September 22, 1776 — 'I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country.' Both Huntington and Hale are buried at the Nathan Hale Cemetery in Coventry.

narrative_blocks.header: Almost succeeded Wheelock as Dartmouth president, 1780
narrative_blocks.body: After the death of Rev Eleazar Wheelock — Dartmouth's first president — communications were made to Huntington that gave him reason to expect election as Wheelock's successor. He actually made the journey to Hanover NH before declining. Yale conferred a D.D. honoris causa on him that same year, and he was elected a Yale Trustee 1780-1788.

narrative_blocks.header: Preached Universalism quietly for two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Old parishioners later claimed 'Dr Huntington preached good Universalism for twenty years, but in such a way that he was not suspected, except by those who relished it.' His preface to Calvinism Improved acknowledged the work 'contained a small part of a system of divinity which the author has been meditating more than twenty years' — his Coventry parish never knew their pastor's actual theology.

narrative_blocks.header: Open-air ordination at decayed Coventry meeting house
narrative_blocks.body: Installed June 29, 1763 as pastor of the First Church of Coventry, where the previous meeting house had been suffered to go to decay. The ordination services were held in the open air, whether because the building was too small or too dilapidated to be safe never made clear. Within a few years he led the parish to build the best house of public worship in the region at a cost of five thousand dollars.

death.notes: Per Huntington Family book: 'his widow [died] April 16, 1843' — five months after Amasa. Per joint memorial inscription: 'aged 69 years' (so birth corrected per Coventry CT Aug 22, 1774).

burial.plot_notes: REINTERRED December 10, 1870 from original Wilkes-Barre PA burial. Joint memorial with husband Amasa Jones. Reinterment likely facilitated by son Matthew Hale Jones (1811-1883), lawyer at Easton — deferred.

marriages.notes: Per Huntington Family in America 1915: m. 'Nov. 9, 1794, Amasa Jones of Coventry... son of Col. Joel Jones, of Hebron, Conn.' 9 children; 6 living as of 1858 per the book. Only TD0239 Judge Joel Jones built this stage. 8 children deferred (Talcott gen 7 cohort): Rev Joseph Huntington Jones DD 1797-1868 (Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia, Harvard 1817), Fanny Huntington 1799-1893 (Easton PA), Margaret Emeline 1801-1806 child death, Maria Allis 1803-1884 (m. William Allis Westfield NY), Eliza Wright 1805-1854 St Louis (m. Joseph J. Wright US Army surgeon), Dr Samuel H. Jones 1808-1864, Mary Joanna Harris 1809-1837 (m. Rev O. Harris), Matthew Hale Jones 1811-1883 Easton PA lawyer (→ daughter Elizabeth Huntington Jones Kirkpatrick m. US Congressman William Sebring Kirkpatrick).

narrative_blocks.header: Family moved Coventry Connecticut to Wilkes-Barre 1816
narrative_blocks.body: Born Coventry CT August 22, 1774 to Rev Joseph Huntington and his Talcott-descendant second wife Elizabeth Hale Huntington. Married Amasa Jones of Coventry — son of Col Joel Jones of Hebron CT — November 9, 1794 and bore nine children where her father preached. In 1816 the family relocated to Wilkes-Barre PA in the Wyoming Valley.

narrative_blocks.header: Reinterred Easton Pennsylvania twenty-seven years later
narrative_blocks.body: Died Wilkes-Barre PA April 16, 1843 age 68, five months after her husband Amasa. Both were originally buried Wilkes-Barre. December 10, 1870 — twenty-seven years after her death — both bodies were transferred to Easton Cemetery PA, where son Matthew Hale Jones lived as a lawyer.

narrative_blocks.header: Half-sister of Ohio Governor Samuel H. Huntington
narrative_blocks.body: Through father Rev Joseph Huntington's first marriage to Hannah Devotion, she had a half-brother Samuel H. Huntington (1765-1817) who would become the third Governor of Ohio 1808-1810 and chief justice of the Ohio Supreme Court. He was nine years her senior; she was the first of Joseph's nine M2 children with her Talcott-descendant mother.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — pioneer-era family cemetery; same cemetery as wife Mary Tyler Morse Huntington (I01146) and son Henry Augustus Huntington (TD0263).

career.notes: Per Huntington Family in America 1915 record: 'He had spent some portion of his life on the sea.' Specific maritime career details not documented.

Removed from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana 1819 — joining the first Yankee wave to the newly-admitted state (Indiana statehood 1816). 'A man honored by his fellow citizens with frequent testimonials of their confidence' per the Huntington Family in America 1915 record. Died at his Shelby County residence July 20, 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Removed 1819 from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana
narrative_blocks.body: Removed from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana in 1819 — joining the first Yankee wave to the newly-admitted state (Indiana statehood 1816). His six children all born between 1811 and 1825 followed him west; he died at his Shelby County residence July 20, 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Spent some portion of his life at sea
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Huntington Family in America 1915 record: 'He had spent some portion of his life on the sea' before settling in Indiana as a respected citizen. Honored by fellow citizens with frequent testimonials of their confidence in his character.

bio.bio_blurb: Long-lived last survivor of her family

narrative_blocks.header: She outlived every one of her siblings
narrative_blocks.body: Of the family she was born into she lived longest by far — five of her siblings died in childhood, and she outlasted the last of the survivors by more than thirty years, reaching ninety-three.

parents.father_research_notes: Lieut. Samuel Hale Jr. (1645-1711) of Wethersfield CT, son of Samuel Hale Sr. who came from Wales c.1637 and served in the Pequot War; SV gives 'Samuel, Jr., by his wife Mary Edwards.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Edwards (first wife of Samuel Hale Jr., m. 1679, d. Dec 26, 1682).

bio.title: Capt.
bio.bio_blurb: Glastonbury patriarch married to a Talcott

narrative_blocks.header: His family settled Glastonbury for generations
narrative_blocks.body: A Glastonbury man through and through, he married Sarah Talcott and raised eight children there; one son became a Revolutionary War captain. The Hales were remembered locally as people of large stature and great strength.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

will.summary: Will of Lt. Benjamin Talcott, Glastonbury CT. Wife: £80 + use of parlor, cellar, garret, ovens, gardens, one-third of orchard and land west of highway to great river — during widowhood only. Son Benjamin: lands in Bolton + 124 acres Hebron (bought of Joseph and John Phelps). Son John: 300 acres Hebron (west of Jacob Root), 50 acres Hebron (north of Richard Curtis), one yoke of steers, £10 moveable, + £15 from Elizur. Son Samuel: south half of home lot, 153 acres beyond Black Ledge River (Glastonbury/Hebron border), half undivided Glastonbury lands, smith-shop with all smithery tools, longest gun, half team. Son Elizur: north half of home lot with all buildings (except smith-shop), 60 acres Ash Swamp Glastonbury, 96 acres near bare hill Glastonbury, other half undivided lands, newest gun, half team — he to pay John £15. Daughter Sarah: £100. Daughter Hannah: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Daughter Mehitabel: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Executors: son-in-law Jonathan Hale and son Benjamin Talcott. Witnesses: Samuel Smith Jr., Nathaniel Talcott, Thomas Wells. Estate inventory: £2563 15s. 8d.
will.note: Will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.106-109.

cross_connections.display_label: Glastonbury homestead passed intact to him 175 years later.

narrative_blocks.header: His house bore bullet marks until 1854
narrative_blocks.body: He built a fortified wooden house on Main Street, Glastonbury in 1699 — a refuge from Indian attacks for the surrounding community. The house bore bullet marks until it was taken down soon after 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Sarah died in childbed at thirty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister, granddaughter of original 1636 Hartford settler John Hollister, married him in 1699 and bore him eight children. She died in childbirth at the homestead October 15, 1715.

narrative_blocks.header: Sons signed Bolton's 1731 founding patent
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons Benjamin and Nathaniel were listed as Bolton Proprietors in the 1731 patent, signed by their cousin Governor Joseph Talcott. A third son Elizur later chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Wright cousins married two Talcott siblings
narrative_blocks.body: His son Elizur married Ruth Wright; his daughter Mehitabel married Ruth's first cousin Hezekiah Wright. Two Wright cousins thus married two Talcott siblings, weaving the families together.

narrative_blocks.header: Farm in family from 1643 through 1876
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John Talcott had bought the Glastonbury farm from Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve in 1643. The land was still owned by descendant Jared G. Talcott in 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in childbed; the baby followed
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister married Deacon Benjamin Talcott and died in childbed at Glastonbury in 1715, at thirty-eight. The daughter whose birth she did not survive, Abigail, lived only eighteen days more.
